sections.title: Expressing the future: will/won't and be going to

content.description: There is often little difference between **will** and **be going to** when talking about the future. In many cases, the choice is simply a matter of style: **will** tends to sound more formal, while **be going to** is more typical of everyday spoken English.

content.subtitle: Use
items:
Facts about the future: The new café **will** open on the high street next month. (more formal)
The new café **'s going to** open on the high street next month. (more informal)

Predictions not based on present evidence: One day, people **will** be able to travel to space as tourists. (more formal)
One day, people **are going to** be able to travel to space as tourists. (more informal)

Decisions made at the moment of speaking: I've made up my mind! I **won't** buy a new laptop just yet. (emphasising the decision)
I've made up my mind! **I'm not going to** buy a new laptop just yet. (emphasising the intention)

content.description: In certain situations, **will** is the preferred choice rather than **be going to**.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Offers and suggestions: I**'ll** carry your bags to the car, if you like.
Requests: **Will** you help me carry these boxes upstairs?
Most first conditional sentences: If we leave now, we**'ll** catch the last train.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: With offers and suggestions in the question form, we do not use **will** with I and we. We use **shall**.
items: ✓ **Shall** I carry your bags to the car?
✗ **Will** I carry your bags to the car?

content.description: In other situations, **be going to** is the more natural choice over **will**.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Plans and intentions (which you already have when you speak): I**'m going to** run a marathon next spring!
Predictions based on present evidence: Look at those clouds! It **'s going to** rain in a few minutes.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Arrangements made before the moment of speaking: Are they delivering the new furniture on Thursday?

content.subtitle: Use
items: Timetables, arrangements and fixed events: The first train to Edinburgh **leaves** at 7.30 tomorrow morning.
After if in first conditional and zero conditional sentences: If the weather stays warm all week, what are we going to do at the weekend?
After certain time expressions: We'll know the results as soon as we get back to the hotel.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions which are completed some time between now and a point in the future: I'll have finished cooking dinner by the time you get home.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions in progress at a point in the future: This time tomorrow, I'll be lying on a beach in Spain.
Habits or repeated actions at a point in the future: In the future, we'll all be ordering our shopping online using voice-activated devices.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions in progress up to a point in the future: At nine o'clock tonight, I'll have been waiting for this concert to begin for two whole hours!

content.description: In time clauses, we avoid using **will** or **be going to** directly after certain time words and phrases. Instead, we use a present tense (present simple, present continuous or present perfect) when talking about future events.

content.subtitle: Use
items: when: It'll be amazing **when** the team **wins** / **has won** the championship.
as soon as: Let me know **as soon as** your parcel **arrives** / **has arrived**.
before: It'll be a few more years **before** they **open** / **have opened** the new airport terminal.
after: Let's grab a coffee **after** we **do** / **have done** the shopping.
until / till: The match won't start **until** the referee **does** / **has done** a final check.
while: Think of me **while** you **relax** / **are relaxing** on the beach!
once: We'll stop for a break **once** we **pass** / **have passed** the motorway junction.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Key prepositions of time: **(from)** Monday **to** Friday, **on** Monday, **on** my birthday/New Year's Day/etc, **in** March, **on** October 15th, **in** 2019, **in** (the) autumn, **at** half past seven, **in**/**for** an hour, **at** the moment, **in** the morning/afternoon/evening, **at** night, **in**/**on** time, just **in** time for, **in** the beginning/end, **at** the beginning/end of, **at** the age of, **at** the weekend, **next**/**last** week

Key prepositions of place: turn left **at** a junction, sit **on** sth, go **in(to)** a building, wait **in(side)** a building, arrive **in** Rome/Italy, arrive **at** the airport, **in**/**on**/**at** the corner (of), come/go/walk/etc **to** a place, **next to**/**beside**/**by** the café, **at**/**on** the front/back of, **in front of**/**behind** the hotel, go **out of** a building, go **towards** the town centre, **between** the two shops, **opposite** the bank

content.subtitle: American English notes
items: Speakers of American English do not always use **on** before days of the week.
US: We've got a football match Saturday/**on** Saturday. UK: We've got a football match **on** Saturday.
Speakers of American English often say 'Monday **through** Friday'.
US: I'm going to be away on a school trip Monday **through** Friday. UK: I'm going to be away on a school trip **from** Monday **to** Friday.

sections.title: E: Original key word transformation practice
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it keeps the same meaning as the first.

items.sentence:
Mia began the class project before the lesson on Future time. **GOT**
The class project ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: when we've got/once we've got/after we've got

items.sentence:
Leo began the study group before the lesson on Future time. **SOON**
The study group ...... before the lesson on Future time.

Sara began the exam workshop before the lesson on Future time. **WHILE**
The exam workshop ...... before the lesson on Future time.

Tom began the online lesson before the lesson on Future time. **BEFORE**
The online lesson ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: before they show Titan/before they have shown Titan

items.sentence:
Nina began the school club before the lesson on Future time. **TAKING**
The school club ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: I will be taking/I'll be taking

items.sentence:
Oscar began the revision session before the lesson on Future time. **WATCHED**
The revision session ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: after we have watched/after we've watched

items.sentence:
Iris began the practice notebook before the lesson on Future time. **HAVE**
The practice notebook ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: I will have sent out/I'll have sent out

items.sentence:
Ben began the language lab before the lesson on Future time. **UNTIL**
The language lab ...... before the lesson on Future time.
items.answer: until you do/until you have done

items.sentence:
Lena began the library task before the lesson on Future time. **BY**
The library task ...... before the lesson on Future time.

Marco began the morning tutorial before the lesson on Future time. **ONCE**
The morning tutorial ...... before the lesson on Future time.
